title.zh: 被斩首的印加王将会归来
title.en: The Beheaded Inca King Who Will Return

summary.zh: 安第斯口传的弥赛亚式传说：印加王被西班牙人斩首，头颅埋于地下，身体正朝头颅缓缓生长；待身首重合之日，印卡里归来，天地翻转复序。
summary.en: An Andean oral legend of return: the Inca king was beheaded by the Spaniards and his buried head is slowly growing a new body; when it is whole, Inkarri will come back and the world will turn right again.

description.zh:
“印卡里”一名由克丘亚语的“印加”与西班牙语的“国王”（rey）拼合而成。传说的骨干在各地版本中大体一致：印加王被西班牙人捕获斩首，头颅被埋在地下——有说在利马的宫殿之下，有说在库斯科；但头颅没有死，正朝着躯体缓缓生长，待身首重新合一之日，印卡里将从地下归来，那时天地翻转（克丘亚语“帕查库蒂”），旧的秩序回到人间。阿亚库乔省普基奥的版本还讲他的来历：印卡里是太阳之子，曾把太阳拴在山上，让时日走得更慢，又以神力奠定库斯科。

传说背后叠着两桩史实，口头记忆将其揉成了一个人：1533年阿塔瓦尔帕在卡哈马卡被西班牙人处死——史载死于绞刑，传说却记成斩首；1572年比尔卡班巴的末代印加图帕克·阿马鲁在库斯科广场被公开斩首。可考的行刑与不可考的头颅，在四个世纪的讲述里长成了同一株传说。

这一口头传统直到20世纪50年代中期才见诸文字：作家兼人类学家何塞·玛丽亚·阿格达斯与何塞法特·罗埃尔·皮内达在普基奥采录到最早的几个版本，此后研究者又在阿亚库乔、库斯科、普诺等地录得数十种异文。印卡里由此进入秘鲁思想界，成为20世纪讨论安第斯认同与历史创伤时反复援引的核心意象。
description.en:
The name Inkarri fuses Quechua 'Inka' with Spanish 'rey', king. The spine of the legend is stable across its many versions: the Inca king was captured and beheaded by the Spaniards, and his head lies buried — beneath the palace in Lima, some say, or in Cusco. But the head is not dead. A body is slowly growing back from it, and on the day head and body are whole, Inkarri will return from under the earth; the world will turn over — pachakuti — and the old order come back. The versions from Puquio in Ayacucho add his origins: Inkarri was a son of the Sun who once tethered the sun on a mountain so the days would pass more slowly, and founded Cusco by his power.

Two documented events lie pressed together beneath the story, fused by oral memory into one man: the execution of Atahualpa at Cajamarca in 1533 — by garrote in the records, though the legend remembers a beheading — and the public beheading of Túpac Amaru, last Inca of Vilcabamba, on the plaza of Cusco in 1572. A verifiable execution and an unverifiable buried head grew into a single tale across four centuries of telling.

The tradition reached writing only in the mid-1950s, when the novelist and anthropologist José María Arguedas and Josafat Roel Pineda recorded the first versions at Puquio; researchers have since collected dozens of variants across Ayacucho, Cusco and Puno. Inkarri passed from the villages into Peruvian intellectual life, an image returned to again and again in twentieth-century debates over Andean identity and historical loss.

value.zh: 克丘亚语“印加”+西班牙语“国王”
阿塔瓦尔帕（1533）与图帕克·阿马鲁（1572）之死
value.en: The deaths of Atahualpa (1533) and Túpac Amaru (1572)

value.zh: 1950年代中期，阿亚库乔省普基奥

sources.zh: 何塞·玛丽亚·阿格达斯与何塞法特·罗埃尔·皮内达在普基奥采录的口述版本（1950年代中期）
sources.en: Oral versions recorded at Puquio by José María Arguedas and Josafat Roel Pineda (mid-1950s)

sources.zh: 阿亚库乔、库斯科、普诺等地延续至今的口头流传
sources.en: Continuing oral tradition in Ayacucho, Cusco and Puno

label.zh: 普基奥（首批版本采录地）
label.en: Puquio, where the first versions were recorded
